justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-7, with BR-3/BR-4 covering the cancellation role split). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Double-booking (BR-2) marked invariant: true. Consultation note lock (BR-7) marked invariant: false with nuanced justification distinguishing the timing policy from the immutability concept — acceptable interpretation. Role-based cancellation correctly captured as separate rules for patient (BR-3) and admin (BR-4). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 82 is within the 65-90 range, with clearly stated assumptions about license verification semantics and boundary conditions.

observations.text: Excellent separation of cancellation into two distinct rules (BR-3/BR-4) capturing the patient vs admin role boundary explicitly
12 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum, covering all required scenarios plus additional edge cases like patient self-access and physician onboarding
Rich edge cases section identifying race conditions, timezone issues, license revocation, and boundary conditions
Confidence assumptions clearly note uncertainty about what 'licensed' means in the domain model, as expected by rubric
Ubiquitous language section provides precise domain definitions that ground the business rules

BR-7 (consultation note lock) marked invariant: false — the rubric expects invariant: true for record lock as a clinical record integrity rule. The agent's nuanced justification (timing is policy, immutability concept is invariant) is reasonable but technically a misclassification against the rubric's strict reading
Business impact analysis is thorough, identifying specific risks like race conditions and clock skew that demonstrate deep domain understanding
